coredata.dc:title: Design and synthesis of quasi- spherical fluorescent carbon quantum dots for visualization of latent fingerprints
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Rotti, Rajeshwari B.
Shafi, Sara
Nagaraju, G.
Gouda, Girish M.
Sunitha, D.V.
Darshan, G.P.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel class of material that grabbed many engrossments as a promising light-emitting material. In the present work, environmentally friendly, non-toxic, and fluorescent CQDs were synthesized using organic plant-based extracts, such as Brassica juncea (mustard seeds), Citrus limon (lemon juice), Cuminum cyminum (cumin seeds) and Mangifera indica (mango leaves) by following a hydrothermal route. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) studies revealed a peak at 28.7⁰ corresponding to the (002) plane confirming the formation of CQDs. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images for the synthesized CQDs revealed their average particle size of ∼ 3 nm. The optical energy band gap of the prepared CQDs was found to be in the range of ∼ 3.67 - 3.99 eV. The fluorescence emission spectra revealed an intense and broad peak centered at ∼ 462 nm (blue fluorescence), which is attributed to electronic π-π* transition (CC bond) and n-π* transition (CO bond) on the surface of the CQDs. The lemon juice-sourced CQDs showed a prominent fluorescence intensity as compared to other carbon sources due to the existence of the surface emissive trap state on the surface. The photoluminescence (PL) lifetime of the prepared Citrus limon-based CQDs was ∼ 8.9 ns. The latent fingerprints (LFPs) were visualized using optimized Citrus limon sourced CDs on various porous and non-porous substrate surfaces and found type I, type II, and type III fingerprint details with high sensitivity, selectivity, and without any background hindrance, which, owing to prepared fine powder adheres to the only ridges of the fingerprints. The aforementioned results specifically navigate the applicability of the prepared CQDs in the forensic science field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Fluorescence
Quantum confinement
Latent fingerprints
Hydrothermal method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Visualization of LFPs using prepared CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the carbonization mechanism involved during the formation of CQDs [14,27,38].
(a) PXRD profiles, (b) FTIR spectra, and (c) Raman spectra of the prepared CQDs.
TEM and HRTEM (Inset) images of prepared (a) LJ CQDs, (b) CS CQDs, (c) MS CQDs, and (d) ML CQDs, (e) SAED pattern, and (f) histogram plot of prepared LJ CQDs.
(a) UV-visible absorption spectra, and (b) corresponding Tauc's plots of the prepared CQDs.

(a) PL excitation spectrum of the synthesized LJ CQDs, (b) Emission spectra of the LJ, CS, MS, and ML CQDs excited at 341 nm at RT, and (c) Emission spectra of LJ CQDs recorded at different excitation wavelengths.
Fluorescence decay curves of (a) LJ CQD, (b) CS CQD, (c) MS CQD, and (d) ML CQDs.

LFPS visualized using prepared LJ CQDs on different surfaces, (a) aluminum foil, (b) A4 sheet, (c) glass petri dish, (d) butter paper, (e) blue color stick note, and (f) granite tile under normal light.
Enlarged portions of the visualized LFPs on granite tile show various ridge characteristics.

head.ce:title: Design and synthesis of quasi- spherical fluorescent carbon quantum dots for visualization of latent fingerprints

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eDepartment of Physics, Faculty of Natural Sciences, M. S. Ramaiah University of Applied Sciences, Ramaiah Technology Campus, Bengaluru 560 058, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel class of material that grabbed many engrossments as a promising light-emitting material. In the present work, environmentally friendly, non-toxic, and fluorescent CQDs were synthesized using organic plant-based extracts, such as Brassica juncea (mustard seeds), Citrus limon (lemon juice), Cuminum cyminum (cumin seeds) and Mangifera indica (mango leaves) by following a hydrothermal route. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) studies revealed a peak at 28.7⁰ corresponding to the (002) plane confirming the formation of CQDs. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images for the synthesized CQDs revealed their average particle size of ∼ 3 nm. The optical energy band gap of the prepared CQDs was found to be in the range of ∼ 3.67 - 3.99 eV. The fluorescence emission spectra revealed an intense and broad peak centered at ∼ 462 nm (blue fluorescence), which is attributed to electronic π-π* transition (CC bond) and n-π* transition (CO bond) on the surface of the CQDs. The lemon juice-sourced CQDs showed a prominent fluorescence intensity as compared to other carbon sources due to the existence of the surface emissive trap state on the surface. The photoluminescence (PL) lifetime of the prepared Citrus limon-based CQDs was ∼ 8.9 ns. The latent fingerprints (LFPs) were visualized using optimized Citrus limon sourced CDs on various porous and non-porous substrate surfaces and found type I, type II, and type III fingerprint details with high sensitivity, selectivity, and without any background hindrance, which, owing to prepared fine powder adheres to the only ridges of the fingerprints. The aforementioned results specifically navigate the applicability of the prepared CQDs in the forensic science field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nature provides numerous resources that are found widely and modestly to access as a real-time alternative to chemical reagents. The synthesis of nanomaterials using naturally available resources has been playing a significant role in various investigations and proposes a vast application in many fields, such as tissue engineering, cancer therapy and gene delivery, GO-ITN shield to attenuate X-rays, sensors [1–5], bio-imaging [6], biosensing [7], quantum dot light emitting diodes [8], solar cells [9], optoelectronic devices [10], photocatalysis [11], electrocatalysis [12], and biological applications like in antiviral/ antitumor [13–15], etc. Carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon tubes, diamonds, graphene, and fullerenes, have fascinating properties such as excellent optical properties, non-toxicity, easy surface modification, good biocompatibility, and high conductivity. But, graphene and fullerene are insoluble in solvents, and less fluorescence limits its application [14]. The CQDs exhibit excellent fluorescent properties by absorbing ultraviolet (UV) light and emitting it in the visible light range. Because the surface of CQDs is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups.

Developing environmental friendly, non-toxic CQDs in less time and cost is challenging. Several methods have been adopted to prepare the CQDs, such as microwave, arc-discharge, laser-ablation, hydrothermal, ultrasonication, and electrochemical methods. etc. But microwave technique, arc-discharge, and ultrasonication exhibit some limitations such as scalability, poor control over size, surface functionalize, energy consumption, and poor homogeneity, whereas the laser-ablation method has a complex operation, high cost, and non-uniform particle size [12]. Amongst, the hydrothermal technique is a highly convenient method for the preparation of CQDs because of less cost, requires low preparation temperature, non-toxic, and environmentally friendliness. The typical synthesis procedure involves different reactions during carbonization, like hydrolysis, dehydration, decarboxylation, aromatization, and polymerization [14]. Organic molecules extracted from plants and biomass waste are used as raw materials for the synthesis of CQDs. Plant parts extracts such as fruits, flowers, seeds, leaves, and stems consist of various heteroatoms like nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), fluorine (F), boron (B), and sulfur (S), phytochemicals such as flavonoids, phenolic, polyphenols, saponins, proteins which acts as a surface-functionalize are taken as carbon source as they are low cost, safe, abundant, and eco-friendly [16,17]. Furthermore, the organic precursors also consist of functional groups, such as the carbonyl group (−CC), carboxyl group (−COOH), hydroxyl group (−OH), and amines (−NH). These sources are rich in carbon, and are responsible for fluorescence emission, water solubility, and absorption [18].

The raw materials selected in the present work are based on their various phytochemical properties. Normally, mustard seeds (MS) belonging to the Brassicaceae family, known as Brassica juncea, consist of anti-oxidants and bio-active constituents, like phenolic compound and sulfur-containing compounds like glucosinolates. This is hydrolyzed in the presence of an enzyme myrosinase to form isothiocyanates. The isothiocyanate is a phytonutrient majorly present in MS that has anticancer benefits [19]. Lemon belongs to the Rutaceae family, or the citrus family known as Citrus limon (LJ) has phytochemicals like citric acid, which play a major role in the synthesis of various nanomaterials. For instance, citric acid plays an important role in the enhancement of fluorescence intensity [20]. The cumin seeds (CS) belong to the Apiaceae family known as Cuminum cyminum. CQDs obtained from cumin seeds have good water dispersibility, high fluorescence intensity, etc. CQDs synthesized using cumin seeds/spices are useful for imaging cancer cells in the kidney and limit the further growth of tumors [21]. However, mango leaves (ML) belonging to the family of Anacardiaceae known as Mangifera indica leaves and possess enhanced anti-oxidant properties. Therefore they are used as anticancer and antiallergic agents. Phytochemicals, like Mangifera is a polyphenolic compounds that do not cause cellular toxicity and have good fluorescence with any surface passivation [22]. The chemical composition is present in the organic extracts [23–26]. The organic precursors, like MS, CS, ML, and LJ, majorly contain carbohydrates ,which act as a carbon source. Initially, during the hydrolysis process, the insoluble polysaccharides are converted into soluble monomers, like glucose, fructose, pentose, hexose, etc. [27]. This is followed by the dehydration of these monomers, which can form reactive intermediates, like hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF), furfural, and phenolic compounds with their derivatives [28]. Then, rehydration of these intermediates forms acids, like levulinic acid and formic acid, leading to decarboxylation from carbon dioxide and water. Carbon-rich product formed during hydrothermal process is due to the condensation polymerization specified as aldol condensation of furan derivatives. Polymer products undergo aromatization at different temperatures [29].

Fingerprints are the unique and permanent features of individuals used for identifying criminals in forensic applications. There are many microscopic and spectroscopic techniques used for the identification of fingerprints, amongst the most extensively used inexpensive techniques is the visualization of LFPs by using fluorescent materials are simple and accurate. The powder dusting method is a conventional method used for the development and visualization of LFPs. Till date, there are numerous efforts have been made to develop fingerprints. In literature, it is observed that CeO2:Eu3+, Mg2TiO4: Mn4+, YAlO3:Tm3+ , Mg2SiO4: Eu3+/Tb3+, NaYF4: Yb; Er, SrAl2O4: Eu3+, Dy3+, BaSrGd4O8:Sm3+, and so on [30–36] are used for the development of LFPs. However, these materials are toxic and require UV light to observe fingerprints. In this regard, CQDs can be considered as an alternative non-toxic material that can be used for the visualization of LFPs because of their stable structure, fluorescent and highly reactive materials that can easily bind the ridge of fingerprints [37].

In the present work, we made an effort to prepare the CQDs by hydrothermal method using different plant extracts such as lemon juice (Citrus limon), cumin seeds (Cuminum cyminum), mustard seeds (Brassica juncea) and mango leaves (Mangifera indica leaves). Further, its luminescent characteristics were characterized and compared. The optimized CQDs were used for the development of LFPs, and a thorough analysis was carried out for its possible utilization in forensic applications.

In the present work, the plant-based precursors, such as MS, CS, ML, and LJ were used to prepare CQDs and were purchased from the local market in Bengaluru. The selected MS, CS, and ML were thoroughly washed with deionized water, dried at 100 °C using a hot air oven, and ground into fine powder using agate and mortar. Later, 0.5 g of organic extract powder was taken and dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water. Whereas in the case of LJ 25 mL of LJ was extracted from raw lemon, dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water, and stirred for 30 min using a magnetic stirrer at room temperature (RT). The obtained solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heat treated at 140 °C for 3 h. Finally, the heated autoclave was cooled down to RT. Subsequently, the obtained dark brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 25 min to remove the larger particles, it was filtered and dried. The detailed schematic illustration demonstrates the carbonization mechanism taking place during the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs given in Fig. 1 . The CQDs are formed due to the carbonization phenomenon by undergoing ,hydrolysis, dehydration, decarboxylation, polymerization, or condensation and aromatization processes [14,27,38].

The structural characterization of the prepared CQDs was analyzed via PXRD recorded in Rigaku Smart lab X-ray diffractometer using CuKα X-rays having a λ = 1.54 Å in the range 10–70°. A Perkin Elmer Frontier spectrometer was used to record the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) data to study the functional groups present in the CQDs. The structural defects were analyzed through Raman spectroscopy and recorded by using a Peek seeker Raman spectrometer in the range 300–3000 cm-1. The bandgap of the CQDs was determined by using the absorption spectra recorded by Evolution 201 UV–visible spectrometer (Thermo scientific–make) recorded in the range 200–800 nm. The TEM images were captured by using Titan Themisis 300 kV from Thermo made was used to study the morphological studies. The PL emission, excitation spectra, decay curves, and quantum yield (QY) of CQDs were recorded using the Edinburg FLS1000 spectrofluorometer coupled with an integrating sphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of LFPs using prepared CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The development and visualization of LFPs using CQDs were established by following the conventional powder dusting technique. Initially, the fingerprint donors were identified and washed their hands with soap and water. Then, the washed finger was gently greased using coconut oil, and the finger was stained on different surfaces at nominal pressure so that LFPs were developed. Further, the luminescent CQDs were dusted on the developed LFPs using a smooth feather brush, and excess powder was removed. Then, the obtained fingerprints were photographed using a digital Canon EOS 80D camera under normal light.

TEM images of CQDs prepared using LJ, CS, MS, and ML are shown in Fig. 3 (a–d). It was clear from the images that synthesized CQDs were found to be quasi-spherical particles. The black dot-like structure observed in the micrograph shows the amorphous nature of the carbon [14]. The inset of Fig. 3(a–d) represents the HRTEM images of the CQDs. It was evident from the figure that the interplanar d-spacing value for the (002) plane was found to be 0.22, 0.26, 0.22, and 0.24 nm for prepared LJ, CS, MS, and ML CQDs, respectively. SAED patterns of the synthesized LJ CQDs (Fig. 3(e)) and CS, MS, and ML CQDs (Fig. S1) clearly show the diffused rings that represent the polycrystalline nature of the CQDs. The histograms used for determining the particle size using Image-J software for CQDs prepared by using LJ (Fig. 3(f)) and CS, MS, and ML CQDs (Fig. S2) exhibiting average particle size of ∼ 2.4, 2.3, 2.5, and 2.5 nm for LJ, CS, MS, and ML, respectively.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the UV-visible absorption spectra of the prepared CQDs in the range 200- 800 nm. The spectra revealed the two absorption peaks at ∼ 230 and 330 nm, ascribed to π-π* and n-π* transition of sp2 and sp3 domains, respectively. Also, a peak at ∼ 330 nm indicates the presence of CC and CO bonds [43]. Fig. 4(b) represents the Tauc's plots used to determine the energy bandgap (Eg) of the CQDs established using the following equation [44];
(1)
α h ν = K
(
h ν −
E g
)
2
where K is the Boltzmann constant, hν is photon energy, α is absorption coefficient, and Eg is the optical band gap. The Eg values of the prepared CQDs were estimated and found to be in the range of ∼ 3.67 - 3.99 eV. It was observed that though the preparation conditions, such as time and temperature, were maintained the same, there was a variation in the band gap because of the different precursors used during the preparation. The carbon source and functional groups present on the surface of the CQDs result in a slight variation in the obtained Eg values [45 ]. There are two types of fluorescence mechanisms in CQDs; they are: (i) the fluorescence by conjugated π-domain due to band gap transitions and (ii) emission due to surface defects [46]. Fig. 5 (a) shows the fluorescence excitation spectrum of the prepared LJ CQDs upon 450 nm emission wavelength at RT. The spectrum revealed a broad and intensive peak in the range ∼ 280 – 400 nm centered at ∼ 341 nm. This evidenced that synthesized CQDs can be readily excited in the UV region. The fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized LJ, CS, MS, and ML CQDs under 341 nm excitation wavelength at RT are depicted in Fig. 5(b). The spectra enclosed a broad peak centered at ∼ 462 nm, resulting in blue fluorescence, which was attributed to π-π* electronic transition (CC bond) and n-π* transition (CO bond) on the surface of the CQDs [47,48]. In addition, LJ CQDs showed a prominent fluorescence intensity when compared to CS, ML, and MS CQDs due to the existence of the surface emissive trap state on the surface of the CQDs. Similar PL behavior was observed by Linehan et al. in silicon nanocrystals of different size due to surface trap states [49]. However, Fig. 5(c). shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the LJ CQDs excited at different excitation wavelengths in the range 310 – 380 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity of a peak at 450 nm increased with the increase of excitation wavelength up to 340 nm and diminished with a further increase in excitation wavelength. The observed intensity variation was mainly ascribed to the quantum confinement effect, indicating the variation in the size of the small carbonic core and optical band gap. The average lifetime was estimated using the double-exponential function Y(t) using the following equations [50];
(2)
Y
(
t
)
=
α 1
exp
( −
t
/
τ 1
)
+
α 2
exp
( −
t
/
τ 2
)
(3)
τ ave
=
(
α 1
τ 1 2
+
α 2
τ 2 2
)
/
(
α 1
τ 1
+
α 2
τ 2
)
where the α1 and α2 are the weight of time-resolved of the lifetime decay of τ1 and τ2, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of CQDs consists of a short-lived component τ1 and a long-lived component τ2, respectively. The result reveals that the average fluorescence lifetime of CQDs is about 8.9, 8.09, 7.8, and 7.4 ns for LJ, CS, MS, and ML CQDs, respectively, and the same is shown in Fig. 6 (a–d). The quantum yield (QY) is the ratio of the number of photons emitted to the number of photons absorbed. The QY of the prepared CQDs was calculated using the following equation [50];
(4)
η
=
(
E B
−
E
A
)
/
(
S
A
−
S
B
)
where η is the absolute fluorescence QY of the test sample, S represents the spectral scan of the excitation scattering region of the sample and solvent, and E represents the spectral scan of the emission region of the sample and solvent. A and B represent solvent and sample, respectively. Note that the quantities SA, SB, EA, and EB refer to the integral of the scans. The estimated QY was found to be 14.88 %, 4.72 %, 2.11 %, and 3.22 % for LJ, CS, MS, and ML CQDs, respectively. These results revealed that CQDs prepared using LJ have a longer lifetime and QY when compared to other plant extracts.

A unique fingerprint is created by the skin's ridge pattern on human fingers [51,52]. The ridge patterns can be transferred on any surface through the sweat secreted through the sweat pores [53]. Interestingly, the CQDs are dominating due to their unique properties like low toxicity, low cost, and excellent bio-compatibility with possible application in LFP visualization [54]. Combining the functional group on the surface of CQDs can help to produce the necessary fingerprint biometric characteristics. For instance, Dan et al. [55] developed the LFPs using blue and green-emitting CQDs and found that green light-emitting CQDs are more prominent under UV light. Similarly, Ding et al. [56] developed the LFPs using Fe3O4@SiO2 CD novel composite on many substrates, like plastic, wood, ceramics, etc. To date, several attempts have been made for the visualization of LFPs on various substrate surfaces, namely plastic sheets, painted wood, ceramic tile, aluminum alloy, printed paper, cardboard, coins, and transparent glass under UV-light 365 nm. They have their demerits like high cost and toxicity. Therefore, we made a point to develop and visualize LFPs by using environmentally friendly, less costly, easily available, and efficient fluorescent material. In the present work, LFPs were visualized using prepared LJ CQDs on various surfaces, such as aluminum foil, A4 sheet, glass petri dish, butter paper, blue color stick note, and granite tile by powder dusting technique (Fig. 7 ). As seen from the figure, visualized FPs can exhibit type I, type II, and type III fingerprint details with high sensitivity, selectivity and without any background hindrance. Fig. 8 shows the enlarged portions of the developed fingerprint on the granite tile. As seen from the figure, various fingerprint ridge characteristics, such as island, bifurcation, crossover, termination, core, and scars were visualized in contrast and without any background hindrance. Therefore, the obtained results demonstrated that the prepared LJ CQDs could be used as a potential material for the visualization of LFPs in forensic applications.

Fluorescent CQDs were successfully synthesized using environmentally friendly precursors such as lemon juice (Citrus limon), cumin seeds (Cuminum cyminum), mustard seeds (Brassica juncea), and mango leaves (Mangifera indica leaves) via hydrothermal route. The average quasi-spherical particle size estimated by using TEM images confirms the formation of amorphous CQDs. The Raman spectra reveal the characteristic D band and G band of CQDs, where ID / IG value is less in LJ as compared to CS, MS, and ML CQDs, indicating less lattice disorder and structural defects. Therefore, optimized LJ CQDs were used for developing the LFPs on various surfaces and were visible under normal daylight, which overcomes the limitation of UV source useage for visualization of LFPs. The obtained results show that the non-toxic CQDs are very useful materials for developing LFPs in forensic applications.

Rajeshwari B. Rotti: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sara Shafi: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. G. Nagaraju: Data curation. Girish M. Gouda: Validation, Supervision. D.V. Sunitha: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition. G.P. Darshan: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: I declare that we have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors D.V.S. & R.B.R. thank REVA University for providing the fellowship (RU/R&D/SEED/PHY/2023/36) and CeNSE, Indian institute of science, Bangalore, India for providing a characterization facility through project Ref No.: CeNSE/INUP-i2i/PE_rajeshwari/2022–23

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2025.141674.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lateral flow assemblies and allied application of carbon quantum dots derived from cigarette tobacco in biosensing, anticounterfeiting and fluorescent films: theoretical and experimental overview
